a4abab9b604d1570.summary: Chemical equations represent reactions using symbols and formulas. Reactants â†’ Products. Balancing ensures atom conservation (Law of Conservation of Mass).

chunks.title: Understanding Chemical Equations
chunks.content:
**Chemical Equations** is a fundamental concept in Chemical Reactions and Equations for CBSE Class X Science.

A chemical equation represents a chemical reaction using symbols and formulas.

**General form:**
Reactants â†’ Products

**Example:**
2Hâ‚‚ + Oâ‚‚ â†’ 2Hâ‚‚O

**Balancing:** Count atoms on both sides, add coefficients to equalise. The Law of Conservation of Mass states matter is neither created nor destroyed.

chunks.title: Balancing Chemical Equations
chunks.content:
**Steps to balance:**
1. Write the unbalanced equation (skeleton equation)
2. Count atoms of each element on both sides
3. Add coefficients (numbers before formulas) to balance
4. Never change subscripts (e.g., Hâ‚‚O â†’ Hâ‚‚O, not Hâ‚‚Oâ‚‚)
5. Verify all atoms are equal

**Example:**
Fe + Hâ‚‚O â†’ Feâ‚ƒOâ‚„ + Hâ‚‚
LHS: Fe=1, H=2, O=1
RHS: Fe=3, H=2, O=4
Balance: 3Fe + 4Hâ‚‚O â†’ Feâ‚ƒOâ‚„ + 4Hâ‚‚

**State symbols:** (s) solid, (l) liquid, (g) gas, (aq) aqueous

chunks.title: Types of Chemical Equations
chunks.content:
**1. Combination:** A + B â†’ AB  (2Mg + Oâ‚‚ â†’ 2MgO)
**2. Decomposition:** AB â†’ A + B  (2Hâ‚‚O â†’ 2Hâ‚‚ + Oâ‚‚)
**3. Displacement:** A + BC â†’ AC + B  (Zn + CuSOâ‚„ â†’ ZnSOâ‚„ + Cu)
**4. Double Displacement:** AB + CD â†’ AD + CB  (AgNOâ‚ƒ + NaCl â†’ AgClâ†“ + NaNOâ‚ƒ)
**5. Redox:** Both oxidation and reduction occur

â†“ = precipitate, â†‘ = gas evolved

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Balance: NaOH + Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„ â†’ Naâ‚‚SOâ‚„ + Hâ‚‚O

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Write: NaOH + Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„ â†’ Naâ‚‚SOâ‚„ + Hâ‚‚O
**Step 2:** Na: 1â†’2, balance Na: 2NaOH + Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„ â†’ Naâ‚‚SOâ‚„ + Hâ‚‚O
**Step 3:** H: 2+2=4L â†’ 2R, balance: 2NaOH + Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„ â†’ Naâ‚‚SOâ‚„ + 2Hâ‚‚O
**Step 4:** O: 2+4=6L â†’ 4+2=6R âœ“

**Answer:** 2NaOH + Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„ â†’ Naâ‚‚SOâ‚„ + 2Hâ‚‚O

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** Balance: Fe + Clâ‚‚ â†’ FeClâ‚ƒ

**Answer:** 2Fe + 3Clâ‚‚ â†’ 2FeClâ‚ƒ

---

**Q2.** Balance: CaCOâ‚ƒ + HCl â†’ CaClâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚O + COâ‚‚

**Answer:** CaCOâ‚ƒ + 2HCl â†’ CaClâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚O + COâ‚‚

---

**Q3.** Balance: Câ‚ƒHâ‚ˆ + Oâ‚‚ â†’ COâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚O

**Answer:** Câ‚ƒHâ‚ˆ + 5Oâ‚‚ â†’ 3COâ‚‚ + 4Hâ‚‚O

f98e21f45e91b899.summary: Five types: Combination, Decomposition, Displacement, Double Displacement, Redox. Each has distinct characteristics and examples. Redox involves simultaneous oxidation (gain O/loss H) and reduction (loss O/gain H).

chunks.title: Understanding Types of Chemical Reactions
chunks.content:
**Types of Chemical Reactions** is a fundamental concept in Chemical Reactions and Equations for CBSE Class X Science.

Chemical reactions are classified into five main types based on how reactants transform into products.

**1. Combination:** Two or more substances combine to form a single product.
**2. Decomposition:** A single compound breaks down into simpler substances.
**3. Displacement:** A more reactive element displaces a less reactive one.
**4. Double Displacement:** Exchange of ions between two compounds.
**5. Redox (Oxidation-Reduction):** Simultaneous oxidation and reduction.

chunks.title: Combination, Decomposition, and Displacement Reactions
chunks.content:
**Combination Reaction:**
A + B â†’ AB
â€¢ 2Mg + Oâ‚‚ â†’ 2MgO (magnesium burns with dazzling white light)
â€¢ CaO + Hâ‚‚O â†’ Ca(OH)â‚‚ (slaked lime, exothermic)

**Decomposition Reaction:**
AB â†’ A + B
â€¢ 2Hâ‚‚O â†’ 2Hâ‚‚ + Oâ‚‚ (electrolysis of water)
â€¢ CaCOâ‚ƒ â†’ CaO + COâ‚‚ (thermal decomposition)
â€¢ 2AgCl â†’ 2Ag + Clâ‚‚ (photochemical, white to grey in sunlight)

**Displacement Reaction:**
A + BC â†’ AC + B
â€¢ Zn + CuSOâ‚„ â†’ ZnSOâ‚„ + Cu (blue solution turns colourless)
â€¢ Fe + CuSOâ‚„ â†’ FeSOâ‚„ + Cu (iron nail brown coating)

chunks.title: Double Displacement and Redox Reactions
chunks.content:
**Double Displacement:**
AB + CD â†’ AD + CB
â€¢ AgNOâ‚ƒ + NaCl â†’ AgClâ†“ + NaNOâ‚ƒ (white precipitate)
â€¢ Naâ‚‚SOâ‚„ + BaClâ‚‚ â†’ BaSOâ‚„â†“ + 2NaCl (white precipitate)

**Redox (Oxidation and Reduction):**
Oxidation: Gain of oxygen OR Loss of hydrogen
Reduction: Loss of oxygen OR Gain of hydrogen

â€¢ ZnO + C â†’ Zn + CO
  ZnO â†’ Zn (reduction: loss of oxygen)
  C â†’ CO (oxidation: gain of oxygen)

**Corrosion:** Redox reaction where metal reacts with Oâ‚‚/Hâ‚‚O
**Rancidity:** Oxidation of oils/fats (avoided by antioxidants)

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** What type of reaction is: 2Pb(NOâ‚ƒ)â‚‚ â†’ 2PbO + 4NOâ‚‚ + Oâ‚‚?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** One reactant breaks into multiple products
**Step 2:** Heat is required (thermal decomposition)
**Step 3:** Brown fumes of NOâ‚‚ observed

**Answer:** Thermal Decomposition Reaction

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** Classify: CaO + Hâ‚‚O â†’ Ca(OH)â‚‚

**Answer:** Combination reaction

---

**Q2.** Classify: Zn + 2HCl â†’ ZnClâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚

**Answer:** Displacement reaction (Zn more reactive than H)

---

**Q3.** In CuO + Hâ‚‚ â†’ Cu + Hâ‚‚O, identify oxidation and reduction.

**Answer:** CuOâ†’Cu = reduction (loss O). Hâ‚‚â†’Hâ‚‚O = oxidation (gain O).

d6f4d35bc1606c11.summary: Oxidation = gain of oxygen/loss of hydrogen. Reduction = loss of oxygen/gain of hydrogen. Redox reactions occur simultaneously. Oxidising agent gives oxygen, reducing agent removes oxygen.

chunks.title: Understanding Oxidation and Reduction Reactions
chunks.content:
**Oxidation and Reduction Reactions** is a fundamental concept in Chemical Reactions and Equations for CBSE Class X Science.

**Oxidation:** Gain of oxygen OR Loss of hydrogen
**Reduction:** Loss of oxygen OR Gain of hydrogen

These always occur together â€” hence **Redox** (Reduction-Oxidation).

**Example:**
CuO + Hâ‚‚ â†’ Cu + Hâ‚‚O
CuO â†’ Cu: Reduction (lost oxygen, Hâ‚‚ is reducing agent)
Hâ‚‚ â†’ Hâ‚‚O: Oxidation (gained oxygen, CuO is oxidising agent)

chunks.title: Oxidising and Reducing Agents
chunks.content:
**Oxidising Agent (Oxidant):**
Substance that gives oxygen OR removes hydrogen
Gets reduced itself

**Reducing Agent (Reductant):**
Substance that removes oxygen OR gives hydrogen
Gets oxidised itself

**Examples:**
â€¢ ZnO + C â†’ Zn + CO: C is reducing agent, ZnO is oxidising agent
â€¢ MnOâ‚‚ + 4HCl â†’ MnClâ‚‚ + 2Hâ‚‚O + Clâ‚‚: MnOâ‚‚ oxidises HCl to Clâ‚‚

**Common oxidising agents:** KMnOâ‚„, Kâ‚‚Crâ‚‚Oâ‚‡, Hâ‚‚Oâ‚‚, Oâ‚‚, HNOâ‚ƒ
**Common reducing agents:** Hâ‚‚, C, CO, NaBHâ‚„, metals (Zn, Fe)

chunks.title: Corrosion and Rancidity
chunks.content:
**Corrosion:**
Slow destruction of metals by reaction with Oâ‚‚, Hâ‚‚O, acids.
â€¢ Rusting: Fe + Oâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚O â†’ Feâ‚‚Oâ‚ƒÂ·xHâ‚‚O (rust)
â€¢ Tarnishing: Silver darkens (Ag + Hâ‚‚S â†’ Agâ‚‚S)

*Prevention:* Painting, oiling, galvanising (Zn coating), alloying

**Rancidity:**
Oxidation of oils/fats â†’ unpleasant smell/taste

*Prevention:*
â€¢ Air-tight containers
â€¢ Refrigeration
â€¢ Antioxidants (BHA, BHT, Vitamin E)
â€¢ Nitrogen flushing in packages

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** In the reaction 2Mg + Oâ‚‚ â†’ 2MgO, identify oxidation, reduction, agent.

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Mg + Oâ‚‚ â†’ MgO: Mg gains oxygen â†’ Mg is OXIDISED
**Step 2:** Oâ‚‚ is reduced (gains electrons from Mg)
**Step 3:** Oâ‚‚ is OXIDISING AGENT, Mg is REDUCING AGENT

**Answer:** Mg oxidised, Oâ‚‚ reduced. Oâ‚‚ = oxidising agent, Mg = reducing agent.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** Identify oxidised and reduced: 2Cuâ‚‚O + Cuâ‚‚S â†’ 6Cu + SOâ‚‚

**Answer:** Cuâ‚‚Oâ†’Cu: reduction (loss O). Cuâ‚‚Sâ†’SOâ‚‚: oxidation (gain O).

---

**Q2.** What is the oxidising agent in MnOâ‚‚ + 4HCl â†’ MnClâ‚‚ + 2Hâ‚‚O + Clâ‚‚?

**Answer:** MnOâ‚‚ (gives oxygen to HCl, itself reduced to MnClâ‚‚).

---

**Q3.** How is galvanisation an anti-corrosion method?

**Answer:** Zn coating on Fe. Zn oxidises first (sacrificial protection), protecting Fe from rusting.
